coredata.dc:title: Nickel/nickel oxide nanocomposite particles dispersed on carbon quantum dot from caffeine for hydrogen release by sodium borohydride hydrolysis: Performance and mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Onat, Erhan
İzgi, Mehmet Sait
Şahin, Ömer
Saka, Cafer
coredata.dc:description: The political and economic problems caused by the limited use of fossil fuels are another important reason that leads people to alternative energy sources. Compared to other energy sources, hydrogen has the potential to be the energy source of the future. In this study, the preparation of Ni/NiO doped caffeine-based carbon dot(CCQD) for efficient hydrogen (H2) production from the hydrolysis of sodium borohydride (NaBH4) is included the fabrication of nanoscale caffeine-based carbon quantum dot, the preparation of the CCQD-supported Ni/NiO catalyst as boron-free and the preparation of the CCQD-supported Ni/NiO catalyst by hydrothermal treatment in ethanol solvent (NiO@ CCQD-HT (EtOH)). The HGR values obtained with NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) was 16,819 ml min−1gcat −1. Moreover, the turnover frequency (TOF) value for the NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) was found as 2315 h−1. The activation energy (Ea) for the NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) catalyst was 39.48 kJmol−1.

head.ce:title: Nickel/nickel oxide nanocomposite particles dispersed on carbon quantum dot from caffeine for hydrogen release by sodium borohydride hydrolysis: Performance and mechanism

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The political and economic problems caused by the limited use of fossil fuels are another important reason that leads people to alternative energy sources. Compared to other energy sources, hydrogen has the potential to be the energy source of the future. In this study, the preparation of Ni/NiO doped caffeine-based carbon dot(CCQD) for efficient hydrogen (H2) production from the hydrolysis of sodium borohydride (NaBH4) is included the fabrication of nanoscale caffeine-based carbon quantum dot, the preparation of the CCQD-supported Ni/NiO catalyst as boron-free and the preparation of the CCQD-supported Ni/NiO catalyst by hydrothermal treatment in ethanol solvent (NiO@ CCQD-HT (EtOH)). The HGR values obtained with NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) was 16,819 ml min−1gcat −1. Moreover, the turnover frequency (TOF) value for the NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) was found as 2315 h−1. The activation energy (Ea) for the NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) catalyst was 39.48 kJmol−1.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The materials used in the supported catalyst structure have positive effects on the catalytic activity with their porous structures, surface acidity and active substrate surface area. One of the most important advantages of the use of supported catalysts is its more economical and environmentally friendly features. Environmental friendly materials such as activated carbon, graphene oxide, and magnetic material are widely used as support. The binding of the catalytic active site on the structure used as the support material prevents the aggregation of the catalytic particles. Thus, the prevention of agglomeration positively affects the catalytic performance of the catalyst. Many materials such as polymers, graphene oxide, activated carbon, titanium dioxide, silica, zeolites, and alumina are used as support materials [1–3].

With their excellent optical properties, various surface functional groups, and unique structural and physicochemical properties such as acting as electron donors, carbon quantum dots are increasingly used in catalytic applications as one of the important members of carbon material in recent years [4–6]. In addition, the low cost, easy synthesis and non-toxicity of carbon quantum dots can be considered important advantages for practical application [7,8]. At the same time, hybrid composites of carbon quantum dots with other nanomaterials in catalytic applications have attracted great interest from researchers today [9–13]. In addition, with their large specific surface area and strong complexing interaction between surface groups and many metal ions, carbon quantum dots can be considered a very important material to support catalysts for catalytic applications [14,15]. Currently, excellent carbon quantum dots are prepared from many inexpensive organic small molecules without the use of toxic organic reagents [16–19].

Caffeine (1,3,7-trimethylxanthine; Caf), a white, water-soluble compound found in many plant species, is widely used in the food and pharmaceutical industries. Caffeine has emerged as a natural, green, cheap and effective solid catalyst in various application areas, with advantages such as being environmentally friendly, soft, easily available, non-toxic and biodegradable [20].

The political and economic problems caused by the limited use of fossil fuels are another important reason that leads people to alternative energy sources. In the case of using fossil fuels as an energy source, carbon dioxide(CO2) and some other harmful products are released to nature as the final product. These problems negatively affect all living things in the world, especially humans [21]. Compared to other energy sources, hydrogen has the potential to be the energy source of the future. Hydrogen is converted into energy in systems called fuel cells. This situation enables hydrogen to be defined as an “energy carrier”. NaBH4 is one of the most important metal hydrides in which hydrogen is stored. This importance is due to the properties of the compound. NaBH4 is a compound that theoretically has a hydrogen storage capacity of 10.8 % by mass. Its melting temperature is 400 °C, and it can be used stably up to 315 °C without decomposition [22]. One of the important advantages of NaBH4 is that it is possible to recover hydrogen as well as hydrogen in its structure by dehydrogenation (Eq. (1)).
(1)
NaBH 4
+
2H 2
O → 4
H 2
+
NaBO 2

To date, many heterogeneous catalysts containing metallic nanoparticles or homogeneous catalyst systems have been used for rapid H2 production from NaBH4 [23–28]. However, their use in metallic nanoparticles, especially noble metals, is largely limited due to their high cost and limited reserves. In addition, non-noble metal catalyst systems such as nickel(Ni) [29] and cobalt(Co) [30] have also been used for the hydrolysis of NaBH4. However, extensive morphological and interfacial modifications are required for such catalysts to be more effective due to their inert catalytic nature. Moreover, pure metals and their derivatives still face the problems of poor dispersion and easy aggregation.

In recent years, one of the important methods used in the production of catalysts is the use of supported catalysts [31]. While the support materials increase the stability of the catalysts, on the other hand, they provide a regular distribution on a larger surface. Van der Waals interactions, electron transfers, different interphase surface structures, and adhesion interactions occur between the support material and the active catalyst [32,33].

The performance of catalytic activities can be increased by using environmentally friendly, low-cost and very small-structured CCQDs as a support material in catalyst applications using the hydrothermal method. In addition, the use of solvent systems such as methanol, ethanol(EtOH) and water, which have different physicochemical properties, which can significantly affect morphological properties, in catalyst synthesis has a positive effect on catalytic activity [34]. No study has been found based on the use of carbon quantum dots obtained from caffeine material as a support for nickel catalyst for the production of H2 from NaBH4 hydrolysis. This study involves the first generation of carbon quantum dots obtained from caffeine by a hydrothermal process with Ni metal as a support material to release H2 from the hydrolysis of NaBH4. Characterization of the samples was examined in detail with ultraviolet, fluorescence, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), fourier transform ınfrared spectroscopy(FTIR), nitrogen adsorption/desorption, inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses.

NaBH4 was supplied from Merck (Germany). sodium hydroxide(NaOH), nickel (II) chloride hexahydrate and caffeine were supplied from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). EtOH and methanol were supplied from Tekkim (Turkey).

Surface nanoscale properties of the samples were analyzed with Hitachi High Tech HT7700 model TEM. The crystal structures of the samples were examined with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 A) with the PANalytical Empyrean brand XRD device between 2θ = 10° and 80°. Active groups of the samples were determined with a Nicolet 520 brand FTIR. BET and BJH methods at −196 °C with nitrogen adsorption/desorption (Quantachrome Nova 2200e) were used for pore properties of samples. The elemental components of the samples were investigated with a ZEISS Sigma 300 brand energy dispersive spectroscopy device. Additionally, an Agilent 7800 brand inductively coupled plasma device was used to measure the Ni metal content in the samples. Bond energies and mechanism examination of the samples were carried out using XPS (Specs-Flex) with monochromatic Al Ka radiation. Absorption properties of CCQD samples by Perkin Elmer LS 55 UV–Vis/fluorescence spectrophotometer were analyzed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CCQD and CCQD-supported Ni catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of CCQD by hydrothermal method, a certain amount of caffeine and pure water were left in a furnace. For hydrothermal temperature, 120, 150, 165 and 180 °C were used. 2, 4, 8 and 16 h were preferred for hydrothermal waiting times. Optimum hydrothermal production conditions were determined according to the photoluminescence effect of caffeine kept in the oven at a certain waiting time and temperature under 360 nm. After determining the optimum hydrothermal production operating conditions, the obtained CCQD sample was filtered by a 0.45 μm filter via pressure filtration. Subsequently, this CCQD sample at 10,000 rpm was centrifuged for 60 min. The resulting CCQD was stored for later use. Catalysts obtained by co-precipitation, impregnation and hydrothermal methods were named Ni-CCQD, Ni-caffeine and NiO-CCQD-HT, respectively. Ni(0) was used for comparison purposes. Also, Ni catalysts prepard in water, methanol and ethanol by hydrothermal method were named NiO-CCQD-HT(H2O), NiO-CCQD-HT(methanol) and NiO-CCQD-HT(EtOH), respectively.

For the production of CCQD-supported Ni catalyst, 1, 2, 3, 5 and 7 mmol were used for the specific CCQD ratio versus Ni metal ratio. Catalyst syntheses were carried out using 29.48, 58.96, 88.44, 147.4 and 206.36 mg of Ni metal atoms, corresponding to the amount of 931 mg of CCQD. Three different methods were used for catalyst synthesis, including hydrothermal, impregnation, co-precipitation loading. 10 mmol NaBH4 (10 mL) was used for Ni reduction. The Ni atom species in 36–40 h began the precipitation process. Complete precipitation of the nickel species was achieved by centrifuging at 10,000 rpm for 45 min. Additionally, water, methanol and EtOH solvents were used separately in the preparation of the CCQD-supported Ni catalyst.

A setup including a water bath, jacketed tube, magnetic stirrer and gas burette was used to carry out the hydrolysis of NaBH4 using a CCQD-supported Ni catalyst. In the experimental stage, a certain amount of catalyst (30 mg) and an amount of NaOH were put in a reaction flask. After this process, 4 % NaBH4 was added to the flask in a water bath at a certain temperature. The volume of released H2 was determined with an inverted scale burette using the classical water shift method. The effect of catalyst amount, NaBH4 concentration effect and temperature effect were investigated. H2 generation rate (HGR, ml min−1 gcat−1) and TOF was available for the catalyst performance. The reusability of was examined with six consecutive cycles. After each reusability test, the majority of the liquid fraction (90 %) by decantation was removed and repeatability experiments were performed with the addition of new NaBH4.

Fig. 1 shows images under daylight and ultraviolet light for CCQD production from caffeine by hydrothermal treatment. As can be seen in Fig. 1b, all samples fluorescence under ultraviolet light with a wavelength of 360 nm.

UV (a) and fluorescence absorption spectra (b) of CCQD and Ni@CCQD are shown in Fig. 2a. From the UV–Vis spectra, CCQD and Ni@CCQD have significant absorption in the ultraviolet region (250–300 nm) and the maximum absorption peak is 270 nm due to the n-π* transition of CO and the π-π* conjugate transition of CC [35]. The absorption peak intensity of Ni@CCQD is weaker compared to the CCQD. Fluorescence analysis was performed to determine the photoluminescence properties of CCQDs. Fig. 2(b) gives the photoluminescence(PL) spectra for CCQD and Ni@CCQD samples at 360 nm. Emission for CCQD ranges from 300 to 450 nm with the emission peak centred at 360 nm. PL density is significantly reduced by the incorporation of nickel atoms into CCQD. This confirms the increased quantum excess of the synthesized Ni@CCQD sample. This result indicates both the bonding of Ni atoms to the CCQD surface and the narrowing of the electron transitions of Ni atoms on the CCQD surface.

The crystal structures of the samples were examined with XRD between 2θ = 10° and 80°. Typical XRD patterns of Ni (0), Ni@Caf, Ni@CCQD, NiO@CCQD-HT (H2O) and NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) samples are shown in Fig. 3 . All XRD peaks peaked at approximately 44.5°′(112). The strongest peak was obtained with NiO@CCQD-HT (H2O). However, the intensity of this peak is lower in the NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) sample compared to the NiO@CCQD-HT(H2O) sample. The XRD results show that changing solvent environments affect the crystallinity of Ni nanoparticles. Also, the XRD patterns of NiO@CCQD-HT (H2O)) and NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) samples show the presence of two peaks at approximately 2θ =37°(111) and 62°(220) attributable to NiO(PDF#47–1049), respectively [36,37]. In addition, no clear XRD diffraction peak corresponding to CCQDs could be observed, possibly due to the poor crystallinity of CCQDs. There are a similar explanations in the literature [38,39].

BET and BJH methods at −196 °C with nitrogen adsorption/desorption were used for pore properties of samples. The results obtained by performing BET surface area and pore size distribution analyses of Ni (0), Ni@Caf, Ni@CCQD, NiO@CCQD-HT (H2O) and NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) samples given in Fig. 4 and Table 1 . The surface areas of Ni (0), Ni@Caf, Ni@CCQD, NiO@CCQD-HT (H2O) and NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) samples are 19.5224, 6.99, 4.04, 2.41 and 5.10 m2g−1, respectively. With the modifications made, a decrease is observed in both BET surface areas and pore diameters. At the same time, the BET surface area of the NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) sample increased from 2.41 m2g−1 to 5.10 m2g−1 compared to NiO@CCQD-HT (H2O) catalyst. As a possible reason for this result, it can be stated that the BET surface increases as ethanol opens the sample pores compared to water. Adsorption/desorption isotherms of Ni (0), Ni@Caf, Ni@CCQD, NiO@CCQD-HT (H2O) and NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) samples in the range of 0.0–1.0 P/P0 shows a large number of mesopores with a type IV hysteresis loop.

Surface nanoscale properties of the samples were analyzed with TEM. TEM images of Ni(0)(a), Ni@caf(b), Ni@CCQD(c), NiO@CCQD-HT(H2O) (d) and NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) (e) samples are shown in Fig. 5 . The mean particle sizes of Ni(0)(a), Ni@caf(b), Ni@CCQD(c), NiO@CCQD-HT(H2O) (d) and NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) (e) samples were found to be 4.74, 16.21, 2.43, 2.52 and 2.58 nm, respectively. In addition, the average particle size distribution for NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) is given in Fig. 5f. As can be understood, the average particle size for CCQD obtained from caffeine by the hydrothermal method is quite low compared to Ni(0) and Ni@caf samples. Such nanolayers can stabilize nickel atoms and overcome the aggregation of nickel atoms. A more homogeneous distribution of Ni atoms on smaller samples may have a positive effect on H2 production.

The elemental components of the samples were investigated with EDS. Fig. 6 shows the EDX plots for NiO@CCQD-HT (H2O) and NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) catalysts. The presence of oxygen(O), carbon(C), nitrogen (N) and Ni atoms in the structure of both catalyst samples indicates that Ni atoms have been successfully charged. When the EDS spectrum of the catalyst synthesized in EtOH medium is examined compared to the catalyst prepared in the water solvent, it is seen that the percentages of O and N atoms decrease, but there is an increase in the percentages of Ni and C elements.

Active groups of the samples were determined with FTIR spectroscopy. FTIR analysis to identify functional groups in the Ni@caf(a), Ni@CCQD(b), NiO@CCQD-HT(H2O) (c) and NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) (d) samples at 650–4000 cm−1 was carried out. (Fig. 7 ) The presence of similar functional groups can be seen in all spectra. However, there is an increase in the peak intensities of NiO@CCQD-HT(H2O) (d) and NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) (e) samples in the range of 1000–1200 cm−1, 1500–1750 cm−1 and 3500–3700 cm−1 indicating the raise of oxygen-containing active groups. In the FT-IR spectrum, the peaks between 1500 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 correspond to Brönsted and Lewis acid regions, which are important for catalytic applications. These results showed that the NiO@CCQD-HT(H2O) and NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) catalysts have significant Lewis acid sites. In the FTIR spectrum for the catalyst samples, the bands in the range of 2000–2300 cm−1 can be attributed to multivalent inorganic hydride or oxide components in the samples [40].

In this study, pure Ni (0) catalyst structure was synthesized by co-precipitation for comparison. Then, Ni@caf and Ni@CCQD catalyst structure was synthesized by impregnation. Finally, the Ni@CCQD-HT catalyst structure was synthesized by hydrothermal loading. H2 volumes produced by NaBH4 hydrolysis using Ni (0), Ni@caf, Ni@CCQD (caf), and NiO@ CCQD-HT(H2O) catalysts are shown in Fig. 8 . The HGR values obtained from the hydrolysis of NaBH4 with Ni (0), Ni@caf, Ni@ CCQD (caf), NiO@ CCQD-HT (H2O) catalysts were found as 668, 1459, 3562 and 5127 mLmin−1gcat −1, respectively. HGR values obtained with both Ni@CCQD (caf) and NiO@ CCQD-HT(H2O) catalysts were increased. Therefore, both the CQDs prepared from caffeine and the CQDs prepared from caffeine via hydrothermal process have a significant effect on the catalyst activity. The quite low values average particle sizes were provided by TEM analysis. Therefore, the a more effective performance of the catalyst was provided by preventing of metal aggregation on the support material. It is known that the catalytic activity increases as the size of the metal particles decreases [41,42]. Therefore, the HGR values obtained in this study support this result. At the same time, there is a significant increase in catalytic activity because of the oxidation of CCQD-supported nickel particles. Therefore, both the particle size and the oxidation process affected the catalytic activity. XPS and EDS results also support surface oxidation. These results are compatible with the literature [41,43,44].

Another important parameter measured for catalyst structure efficiency is boron binding. As it is known, boron is also attached to the structure in synthesis studies below 5 °C, where reducing agents such as NaBH4 are used. The aforementioned effect analysis was carried out by providing catalyst synthesis at room temperature and below 5 °C to evaluate the boron binding to all catalyst structures together with hydrothermal optimization. The catalytic reaction hydrolysis data regarding the boron bonding condition for the caffeine carbon dot supported Ni catalyst structure are given in Fig. 9 . The HGR values obtained with Ni and NiB were 6970 and 4360 ml min−1gcat −1, respectively. When Fig. 11 is examined, it will be seen that the catalyst structure that does not bind boron shows a more effective performance.

The efficiency of the Ni@CCQDs catalyst synthesized in different solvent environments were investigated. For this, catalyst synthesis was achieved by using different solvents including methanol and EtOH. H2 volumes produced by NaBH4 hydrolysis with catalysts synthesized in different solvent environments are given in Fig. 10 . HGR values obtained by Ni@CCQDs in H2O, methanol and EtOH were 6970, 14,145 and 16,819 ml min−1gcat −1, respectively. HGR values for the Ni@CCQDs prepared in methanol and EtOH were very higher compared to H2O. The HGR value for the catalyst synthesized in EtOH showed an increase of about 3 times compared to the catalyst synthesized in the water. While the hydrolysis of NaBH4 was completed in 12 min with the catalyst synthesized in pure water, it was completed in 7 and 5 min with the catalyst synthesized in methanol and EtOH. Water, EtOH and methanol were applied to both changes the surface of the carbon dot support and dissolve the nickel nitrate precursor. CCQD-supported Ni catalyst (EtOH) was provided faster H2 conversion with a good modification solvent by supporting the reduction degree of nickel in the ethoxy ions in the solution [45]. That is, a more homogeneous distribution of nickel species can be achieved by promoting the immobilization of nickel atoms on the CCQD support surface in EtOH.

To examine the NaBH4 concentration effect on H2 production, the data obtained from the hydrolysis processes performed with 5 % NaOH at 30 °C with 1 %, 2 %, 4 % and 8 % NaBH4 concentrations are given in Fig. 11 . The HGR values obtained with 1 %, 2 %, 4 % and 8 % NaBH4 concentrations were found to be 9276, 14,748, 16,819 and 11,506 ml min−1gcat −1, respectively. An increase in HGR value is observed with increasing NaBH4 concentration from 1 % to 4 %, while there is a decrease in HGR value with further increases. At the same time, TOF values obtained with 1 %, 2 %, 4 % and 8 % NaBH4 concentrations were found as 1119, 1707, 2315 and 1881 h−1, respectively. Due to the increase in NaBH4, the active sites of this catalyst are gradually occupied by BH4 −. Further increase for NaBH4 will cause an increase in the viscosity of the solution. The high viscosity of this solution adversely affects the interaction between water and the catalyst surface and therefore HGR.

To examine the effect of temperature on H2 production at 30, 40, 50 and 60 °C, the data obtained from hydrolysis processes with 4 % NaBH4 and 5 % NaOH are given in Fig. 12a. The HGR values obtained at 30, 40, 50 and 60 °C temperature values were found as 16,819, 22,406, 35,687 and 52,072 ml min−1gcat −1, respectively. It can be seen from Fig. 12a that the HGR values increase in parallel with the increase in temperature.

The Ea for this catalyst system was determined by the Arrhenius Eq. (2) as given in the formula below.
(2)
Ln
k = ln
A – Ea / RT
where k constant is HGR, Ea is the activation energy, A is the exponential factor, T is solution temperature and R is gas constant. The Ea value from the slope of the graph obtained from the Arrhenius equation is 39.48 kJ mol−1 (Fig. 12b). Table 2 shows the HGR and Ea values obtained with different Ni catalyst systems used for the formation of H2 from NaBH4 hydrolysis in the literature [46–58]. Herein, the Ea value with the NiO@ CCQD-HT (EtOH is much lower than the other catalyst systems.

The repeatability of a catalyst is the premise for industrial use. Fig. 13 shows the six consecutive experiments for the reproducibility of the NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) catalyst. Even after six consecutive hydrolyses, this supported Ni catalyst showed 100 % complete H2 conversion. However, there is a decrease in catalytic activity in repeatability experiments after each use. The decrease in catalyst activity may be attributed to the by-product sodium tetrahydroxyborate formed because of the hydrolysis reaction, accumulating on the catalyst surface and blocking the active sites on the catalyst surface for the reaction. The reason for the decrease in catalytic activity after six cycles can be attributed to the increase in the number of by-product sodium metaborate, which reduces the accessibility of active sites on the catalyst surface or blocks active pores due to the increase in solution viscosity [59]. In addition, the destruction of the Ni/NiO catalyst under the influence of external forces as a result of the continuous reaction of the Ni/NiO catalyst on the CCQD support may also lead to a decrease in the active areas, resulting in a decrease in catalytic activity.

The surface chemical composition of caffeine, NiO@CCQD-HT(H2O) and NiO@ CCQD-HT (EtOH) samples were examined with XPS and the results are shown in Fig. 14 . The investigation spectrum shows the presence of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen atoms in caffeine. However, NiO@CCQD-HT(H2O) and NiO@ CCQD-HT (EtOH) samples detect the presence of nickel atoms as well as carbon, nitrogen and oxygen atoms. In addition, Table 2 gives the surface elemental composition of the caf, NiO@CCQD-HT(H2O) and NiO@ CCQD-HT (EtOH) samples. As can be seen from Table 3 , there is a significant decrease in the N element content of the NiO@CCQD-HT(H2O) and NiO@ CCQD-HT (EtOH) samples compared to the caf sample, while there is an increase in the O content. In addition, the Ni element content of NiO@CCQD-HT(H2O) and NiO@ CCQD-HT (EtOH) samples remained almost the same.

In the NiO@ CCQD-HT (EtOH) sample, C1s (a), N1s (b), O1s (c) and Ni 2p (d) atoms were deconvolved and the results are given in Fig. 15 . XPS split spectra of C1s (Fig. 9a) give four peaks at 284.01, 285.00, 285.98, and 286.98 eV. The peaks located at 284.01, 285.98 and 286.98 eV can be attributed to the CC, CO and CO species [60,61]. Fig. 9b shows the XPS spectrum of N1s at 399.01, 399.98, 401.98, and 403.02 eV. The high-resolution N 1 s spectra peaks at 399.01, 399.98, 401.98, and 403.02 eV can be attributed to pyridinic N, Ni-bonded N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively [62,63]. For O1s there are four peaks of oxygen species at 529.98, 530.97, 532.97 and 533.96 (Fig. 9c). Peaks at 530.97, 532.97 and 533.96 eV can be assigned to OC, adsorbed OH groups and OC-O species, respectively [60,64].

The Ni 2p spectrum (Fig. 10d) shows four peaks at 856, 873 and 879 eV. In Fig. 10d, two peaks at 856 and 873 eV are accordance with Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 energy level, respectively. Meanwhile, the other peak at 879 eV belongs to the corresponding two satellite peaks [65]. Peaks at 856 eV (Ni2p3/2) and 873 eV (Ni2p1/2) represent NiOC [66]. Also, In Ni 2p3/2 spectrums, the peak at 856 eV can be assigned to Ni2+. The existence of Ni2+ is partly due to oxidation when exposed to air [67]. The deposition of Ni on NiO@ CCQD-HT (EtOH) sample was measured by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES). ICP-OES indicates that Ni accumulates 4.14 % by weight in NiO@ CCQD-HT (EtOH).

The efficiency of NiO@ CCQD-HT (EtOH) can be evaluated in three parts. The first step is the use of nanoscale caffeine-based carbon dots. The average particle size of the CQD based on caffeine compared to the Ni (0) and Ni@caf samples is quite low. Such nanolayers can stabilize nickel atoms and overcome the aggregation of nickel atoms. A more homogeneous distribution of Ni atoms on smaller samples may have a positive effect on H2 production. The second step involves the preparation of the CCQD-supported Ni/NiO catalyst as boron-free without reduction with NaBH4. Boron-free catalysts have been studied for different applications. The results obtained showed that the catalysts without boron showed higher activity [68,69]. The third step involves the preparation of the CCQD-supported Ni/NiO catalyst by hydrothermal treatment in EtOH instead of H2O. H2O, EtOH and methanol were applied to both changes the surface of the CCQD support and dissolve the nickel nitrate precursor. The CCQD-supported Ni/NiO (EtOH) exhibited faster H2 conversion. The hydroxyl proton of alcohols can be considered as an electrophilic region with its weakly acidic character. All other aliphatic alcohols except methanol are less acidic than water. In addition, the solvent properties may affect the nucleation during the preparation of the Ni-based catalyst used in this study. Compared to EtOH and methanol, the adsorption of nickel metal species is induced by the stronger OH bond of more polar H2O, creating larger particle sizes. For ethanol, the negative charge is localized on oxygen for ethoxide and methoxy anions for methanol. The acidity character of EtOH is lower than that of H2O due to the electron repulsion of ethoxy species. Oxygen atoms in the solvent structure can coordinate Ni metal species. The oxygen in EtOH has lone electron pairs that can accept protons. Such electron donors can positively affect the hydrolysis kinetics by providing the negatively charged electron needed by the hydrogen atom in the reaction environment. Possible reactions with similar solvent systems in the preparation of metal-containing catalysts are given in the literature. [34,70,71]. Similar reactions can be considered for NiO@ CCQD-HT (EtOH).

The following reactions can be considered for the synthesis of this catalyst in solution [34,72].

In this reaction environment, BH4 − and water molecules immediately adsorb to the surface of NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH). Thus, a complex structure is likely to be formed by interactions between NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH), water and BH4 −. Subsequently, dehydrogenation and the consequent formation of B(OH)4 − ions take place. The electronic structure of Ni atoms could be changed largely due to oxygen atoms on the CCQD-supported Ni/NiO catalyst by XPS, FTIR and EDX analyses. NaBH4 molecules can be adsorbed by Ni metal atoms and activate BH bonds, thereby reducing the activation energy [73,74]. H atoms can be rapidly desorbed from the catalyst surface due to the weak binding energy of Ni metal atoms to H atoms [75]. At the same time, O atoms on catalyst can support easily forming H atoms and OH radicals on the Ni surface as active sites through hydrogen bonds with H2O molecules. Additionally, there may be a strong electrostatic interaction between OH− ions and positively charged Ni species. Thus, the interfaces will contribute to H2 molecules by accumulating abundant H+ atoms and OH- radicals. XRD and XPS analyses confirm the formation of NiO species. Accordingly, more H2 formation sites will be produced with an increasing number of metal-metal oxide interfaces. The preparation scheme of NiO@ CCQD-HT (EtOH) and H2 generation via NaBH4 hydrolysis are shown in Fig. 16 .

Herein, the efficiency of NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) can be evaluated in three parts for efficient hydrogen H2 generation from the hydrolysis of NaBH4.The first step is the fabrication of nanoscale caffeine-based CQD. The average particle size for the CCQDs is quite low compared to the Ni(0) and Ni@caf samples. Such nanolayers can stabilize nickel atoms and overcome the aggregation of nickel atoms. The second step involves the preparation of the CCQD-supported Ni/NiO catalyst as boron-free without reduction with NaBH4. The third step involves the preparation of the CCQD-supported Ni/NiO catalyst by hydrothermal treatment in ethanol solvent instead of water. The characterization of these CCQD-supported Ni/NiO catalysts were carried out by TEM, EDS, FTIR, XPS, BET, XRD and ICP/OES analyses. XPS and EDS plots of NiO@ CCQD-HT (H2O) and NiO@ CCQD-HT (EtOH) catalysts showed the presence of oxygen, carbon, nitrogen and nickel atoms in the structure. The electronic structure of Ni atoms could be changed largely due to oxygen atoms on the CCQD-supported Ni/NiO catalyst by XPS, FTIR and EDS analyses. With this catalyst, excellent catalytic activity was obtained for the production of H2 from NaBH4 hydrolysis. The HGR values for NiO@CCQD-HT catalysts obtained in water, methanol and EtOH were 6970, 14,145 and 16,819 ml min−1gcat −1, respectively. Moreover, the TOF value for the NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) was found as 2315 h−1. The Ea for the NiO@ CCQD-HT (EtOH) catalyst was 39.48 kJmol−1. In addition, this catalyst showed high catalytic activity even after six cycles. The small average particle sizes of the NiO@CCQD-HT (H2O) and NiO@CCQD-HT (EtOH) catalysts used in this study have a positive effect on the activity of the catalyst. Thus, the reaction rates of different applications are likely to be significantly increased by the use of carbon quantum dots as a support in such catalysts.
